subject: Physics
q:
Assume that the earth is a solid sphere of uniform density and a tunnel is dug along its diameter throughout the earth. It is found that when a particle is released in this tunnel, it executes a simple harmonic motion. The mass of the particle is 100 g. The time period of the motion of the particle will be (approximately)

(take g=10 ms-2, radius of earth =6400 km)
question:
Assume that the earth is a solid sphere of uniform density and a tunnel is dug along its diameter throughout the earth. It is found that when a particle is released in this tunnel, it executes a simple harmonic motion. The mass of the particle is 100 g. The time period of the motion of the particle will be (approximately)

(take g=10 ms-2, radius of earth =6400 km)
solution:
The gravitational force acting on a particle of mass m at a distance r (<R) from the centre of Earth is

F=-GM'mr2  ....(1)

where M' is the mass of portion of Earth enclosed by a sphere of radius r concentric with Earth. Here - sign indicates radially inward direction of force.

Taking the density of Earth to be uniform

M'4πr3=M4πR3

⇒M'=Mr3R3   ....(2)

Substituting M' from equation 2 into equation 1:

F=-GMmR3 r

Which is similar to the equation of motion F=-kx with k=GMmR3=mgR (∵g=GMR2)

For such motion, the time period is given by:

T=2πmk

Substituting k from above,

T=2πRg

Putting the values, we get

T=2×3.146400×10310=2×3.14×800≈1 hour 24 minutes

q: A satellite of mass<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>  is launched vertically upwards with an initial speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> from the surface of the earth. After it reaches height <math><mi>R</mi></math> ( <math><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of the earth), it ejects a rocket of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math> so that subsequently the satellite moves in a circular orbit. The kinetic energy of the rocket is ( <math><mi>G</mi></math> is the gravitational constant; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> is the mass of the earth):
question: A satellite of mass<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>  is launched vertically upwards with an initial speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> from the surface of the earth. After it reaches height <math><mi>R</mi></math> ( <math><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of the earth), it ejects a rocket of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>M</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac></math> so that subsequently the satellite moves in a circular orbit. The kinetic energy of the rocket is ( <math><mi>G</mi></math> is the gravitational constant; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>M</mi><mi>e</mi></msub></math> is the mass of the earth):
solution:
-GMeMR+12Mu2=-GMeM2R+12Mv2

 

v=u2-GMeR

Vτ→ Transverse velocity of rocket 
VR→ Radial velocity of rocket

V=GMe2R

M10VT=9M10GMe2R
M10Vr=Mu2-GMeR

Kinetic energy =12M10VT2+Vr2=M2081GMe2R+100u2-100GMeR

=M20100u2-119GMe2R
=5Mu2-119GMe200R

q: Four spheres each of mass m form a square of side d (as shown in figure). A fifth sphere of mass M is situated at the centre of square. The total gravitational potential energy of the system is
question: Four spheres each of mass m form a square of side d (as shown in figure). A fifth sphere of mass M is situated at the centre of square. The total gravitational potential energy of the system is
solution:
The total gravitational potential energy of the system is 

Usystem=U12+U23+U34+U41+U13+U24+U15+U25+U35+U45

=-4Gmmd-2Gmmd2-42GMmd

=-4+2Gm2d-42GMmd

=-Gmd4+2m+42M

subject: Physics
q: Choose the incorrect statement from the following:
question: Choose the incorrect statement from the following:
options: The speed of satellite in a given circular orbit remains constant
For a planet revolving around the sun in an elliptical orbit, the total energy of the planet remains constant
The linear speed of a planet revolving around the sun remains constant
When a body falls towards earth, the displacement of earth towards the body is negligible
solution:
A planet revolves round the Sun by the action of the gravitational force between the sun and the planet. Depending up on the distance from the sun in the elliptic orbit, the speed of revolution of the planet changes.

The formula to calculate the linear speed of a planet in an elliptical orbit can be written as

v=GM2r-1a

From the above expression, it can be concluded that the linear speed of the planet is not constant during its revolution.

subject: Physics
q: A satellite is revolving in a circular orbit at a height h from the carth surface, such that $\mathrm{h}< < \mathrm{R}$ where $\mathrm{R}$ is the radius of the earth. Assuming that the effect of earth"s atmosphere can be neglected the minimum increase in the speed required so that the satellite could escape from the gravitational field of earth is
question: A satellite is revolving in a circular orbit at a height h from the carth surface, such that $\mathrm{h}< < \mathrm{R}$ where $\mathrm{R}$ is the radius of the earth. Assuming that the effect of earth"s atmosphere can be neglected the minimum increase in the speed required so that the satellite could escape from the gravitational field of earth is
solution:
For a satellite orbiting close to the earth, orbital velocity is given by

$v_{0}=\sqrt{g(R+h)}=\sqrt{g R}$

Escape velocity $\left(\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{e}}\right)$ is

$v_{e}=\sqrt{2 g(R+h)}=\sqrt{2 g R} \quad[\because h  < c R$

$\Delta v=v_{e}-v_{0}=(\sqrt{2}-1) \sqrt{g R}$

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following most closely depicts the correct variation of the gravitation potential, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> due to a large planet of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and uniform mass density? (figures are not drawn to scale)
question: Which of the following most closely depicts the correct variation of the gravitation potential, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>)</mo></math> with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> due to a large planet of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and uniform mass density? (figures are not drawn to scale)
solution: At inside points <math><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo><</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>)</mo></math>, <math><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>G</mi><mi>M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><msup><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. So at the centre,   <math><mi>r</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>⇒</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mfrac><mrow><mi>G</mi><mi>M</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfrac><mi> </mi></math>. At outside points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo>></mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>G</mi><mi>M</mi></mrow><mi>r</mi></mfrac></math>. At surface, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mi>G</mi><mi>M</mi></mrow><mi>R</mi></mfrac></math>. So the gravitational potential is non-uniform and negative.

q: Two planets have masses <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> and their radii are a and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math>, respectively. The separation between the centres of the planets is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math>. A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> is fired from the surface of the larger planet towards the smaller planet along the line joining their centres. For the body to be able to reach at the surface of smaller planet, the minimum firing speed needed is :

subject: Physics
q: The acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math>. If the diameter of earth reduces to half of its original value and mass remains constant, then acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth would be :
question: The acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi></math>. If the diameter of earth reduces to half of its original value and mass remains constant, then acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth would be :
solution:
The acceleration due to gravity is related to the mass and the radius of the Earth by the formula:

g=GMR2⇒g∝1R2   ...1

Thus, from equation (1), it follows that

g2 g1=R12R22=R12R122=4⇒g2=4g

subject: Physics
q: A rocket has to be launched from earth in such a way that it never returns. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is the minimum energy delivered by the rocket launcher, what should be the minimum energy that the launcher should have, if the same rocket is to be launched from the surface of the moon? Assume that the density of the earth and the moon are equal and that the earth's volume is <math><mn>64</mn></math> times the volume of the moon.
question: A rocket has to be launched from earth in such a way that it never returns. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi></math> is the minimum energy delivered by the rocket launcher, what should be the minimum energy that the launcher should have, if the same rocket is to be launched from the surface of the moon? Assume that the density of the earth and the moon are equal and that the earth's volume is <math><mn>64</mn></math> times the volume of the moon.
solution:
Mearth=64 Mmoon and Rearth=4 Rmoon
Given: E=G MearthmRearth
Now, for the moon,

 E'=G MmmRm
=GMearth64 mRearth4=G Mearth m16 Rearth=E16
